Build Your AI Brain Bot — Workbook
2
Introduction

How to Use
This Workbook

This workbook walks you through every step of building your AI Brain Bot — from creating accounts to deploying a live Telegram bot that knows your entire body of work. Follow each phase in order. Don't skip ahead.

⏱ Time Budget

Budget a full weekend — Saturday morning to Sunday evening. Beginners realistically need 10–14 hours total. Rushing leads to typos in API keys that take an hour to find.

💡 Pro Tip

Do ALL the account creation (Phase 1) first — even while watching TV. Get every API key saved before you touch any code. That single habit saves hours of friction later.

System Architecture

What You're Building

Your Content (transcripts / docs / notes)
↓
AI Distillation — local, on your machine
↓
Working Brain → Cloudflare R2 (fast search) Big Brain → Supabase (full database)
↓
Groq AI — fast, free 70B model
↓
Your Telegram Bot — live 24/7 on Render
Realistic Timeline

Phase Breakdown

PhaseEst. TimeWhat's Involved
1. Account Setup1–2 hrsCreate all 7 accounts. Verify emails, save API keys.
2. Local Setup1 hrInstall Python + Ollama, verify the local model runs.
3. Brain Building2–4 hrsRun distill_brain.py — depends on your content size.
4. Cloud Upload1 hrUpload brain to Supabase + Cloudflare R2.
5. Bot Deployment1–2 hrsPush to GitHub, configure Render, fix first errors.
6. Test & Keep-Alive1 hrSet up cron job, verify bot answers from YOUR content.
⚠️ Expect Errors

Errors are normal — not a sign you've done something wrong. The most time-consuming part is reading error messages and fixing typos in API keys. Budget time for it.

🐢 The Ollama Bottleneck

If your all.txt file is large (hundreds of pages), brain distillation runs unattended but takes hours. Start it before bed and check it in the morning.

Phase 1

Account Setup

Create all 7 accounts before you touch any code. This is the unsexy part — but getting your credentials organised now saves hours of pain later.

The 7 Accounts You Need
ServiceURLWhat It DoesDone
Supabasesupabase.comStores your Big Brain knowledge database☐
Cloudflarecloudflare.comHosts your fast search index (R2)☐
Groqconsole.groq.comPowers the AI responses (free 70B model)☐
Renderrender.comRuns your bot 24/7 in the cloud☐
GitHubgithub.comStores your bot code for deployment☐
Telegramtelegram.orgWhere your bot lives and responds☐
cron-job.orgcron-job.orgKeeps your Render bot from sleeping☐
My Credentials — Fill This In As You Go
CredentialMy Value
Telegram Bot Tokenpaste here
Groq API Keypaste here
Cloudflare Account IDpaste here
Cloudflare R2 API Tokenpaste here
Supabase Project URLpaste here
Supabase Anon Keypaste here
GitHub Repo URLpaste here
Render App URLpaste here
🔑 Keep Your Keys Safe

Never share your API keys publicly and never paste them into a GitHub file. Always use environment variables — exactly what this guide has you doing in Phase 5.

Build Your AI Brain Bot — Workbook
4
Phase 2

2 Local Setup & Content

1 Install Ollama

Go to ollama.com and download Ollama for your operating system. Once installed, open a terminal and pull your model:

# If you have 8GB RAM: ollama pull qwen2.5:7b # If you have 16GB+ RAM (better quality): ollama pull qwen2.5:14b # If your machine is low on RAM: ollama pull qwen2.5:3b

2 Prepare Your Content

mkdir ~/mybotbrain cd ~/mybotbrain nano ~/mybotbrain/all.txt

Paste ALL your content into all.txt — transcripts, session notes, documents, manuals, anything.

📏 Size Guide

50 pages = about 1 hour to process. 300 pages = 4+ hours. Start with 10 pages to test the whole system works, then run the full content overnight.

Phase 3

3 Build Your Brain

3 Create distill_brain.py

In your ~/mybotbrain folder, create distill_brain.py. Key settings to configure:

MODEL = "qwen2.5:7b" # change to 14b if more RAM CHUNK_SIZE = 8000 # characters per chunk OUTPUT_FILE = "brain_FINAL.jsonl" # your brain file

4 Run the Distillation

ollama serve & python3 distill_brain.py
☕ Go Grab Coffee

This will take a while. You'll see progress like: ⚙️ Processing 1/47... ✅ Chunk 1 complete.

5–6 Sort, Clean & Generate Index

Run the sort/clean script, then generate working_brain_index.json. This is your fast-search index that Cloudflare R2 will serve.

Build Your AI Brain Bot — Workbook
5
Phase 4

4 Cloud Services Setup

7 Set Up Supabase — Big Brain

Create a new project on supabase.com, then run this SQL in the SQL Editor:

CREATE TABLE brain ( id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, text TEXT NOT NULL ); ALTER TABLE brain DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

8 Set Up Cloudflare R2

Create an R2 bucket and upload working_brain_index.json. Set permissions to allow read access.

9 Set Up Groq & Telegram

Generate your Groq API key from console.groq.com. Create your Telegram bot via @BotFather → /newbot.

Phase 5

5 Deploy Your Bot

Set these environment variables in your Render dashboard before deploying:

VariableWhere to Get It
TELEGRAM_TOKENBotFather → /newbot
GROQ_API_KEYconsole.groq.com → API Keys
CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_IDCloudflare dashboard
CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKENAccount API Tokens
SUPABASE_URLSupabase → Settings → API
SUPABASE_TOKENSupabase anon key
R2_BUCKETYour R2 bucket name
R2_OBJECTworking_brain_index
BOT_NAMEYour bot's display name
BOT_PERSONAYour bot's personality description
PYTHON_VERSION3.11
🔑 BOT_PERSONA Example

"You are Joshua Roy, a Results Coach specialising in NLP and Nervous System Reprogramming. You have 12+ years of coaching experience. Be direct, warm and practical. Answer based ONLY on the knowledge provided."

Build Your AI Brain Bot — Workbook
6
Phase 6

6 Test & Go Live

  • Open Telegram and search for your bot by username
  • Send: /start — see welcome message with brain entries count
  • Ask a question from YOUR content
  • Verify answer reflects YOUR content, not generic AI
  • Check Render logs — no errors showing
  • Set up cron-job.org to ping your Render URL every 10 minutes
🎯 What Good Looks Like

Your bot responds to /start and shows: "Brain loaded: 250+ entries." When you ask a question from your content, it gives an answer in YOUR language, YOUR frameworks, YOUR examples.

Troubleshooting Guide

Common Errors & Fixes

ErrorWhat It MeansFix
R2 load failed 403Cloudflare token lacks read accessSet R2 Storage Read — All Resources
RLS policy violationSupabase row-level security blockingRun: ALTER TABLE brain DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
Conflict: terminated by other getUpdatesAnother instance pollingRun deleteWebhook command
model_decommissionedGroq model outdatedChange to: llama-3.3-70b-versatile
Module not foundMissing Python libraryCheck requirements.txt has all packages
Bot not respondingMissing env variable on RenderCheck Render logs for the specific error
📋 First Step Always

When something breaks: open your Render logs. Read the last error message. 90% of issues are either a missing/wrong environment variable or a permissions problem.

Getting Help from AI

Copy your entire bot.py file and paste it into Claude or ChatGPT. Say:

📋 Paste This Prompt

"Here's my bot.py file for a Telegram AI bot. I'm getting [ERROR MESSAGE]. Can you help me fix it?"

Completion Checklist

What You Built
& Learned

  • Distill large content into searchable insights using local AI (Ollama)
  • Store knowledge in a two-tier system: fast index + full database
  • Deploy a 24/7 web service using Render's free tier
  • Integrate Groq's 70B language model via API
  • Keep a cloud service alive with cron-job.org
  • Build a custom AI assistant that answers from YOUR content
  • Set environment variables for secure credential management
  • Debug cloud deployments by reading server logs
Frequently Asked Questions

FAQ

Q1 — My computer doesn't have much RAM. Can I still do this?

Absolutely. If your machine is running lean, pull qwen2.5:3b instead of 7b.

ollama pull qwen2.5:3b

The 3B is lightweight but seriously capable — Qwen 2.5 punches well above its weight class. You're not settling for scraps. Get the bot working first, then upgrade the model later.

💡 Quick Swap

In distill_brain.py, change MODEL = "qwen2.5:7b" to MODEL = "qwen2.5:3b" — that's the only change needed.

Q2 — Can I use other hosting platforms instead?

Yes, 100%. I'm not a computer programmer. I had AI walk me through most of this process, tested different ideas, and scrapped plans that didn't pan out. Some platforms were too complicated. Others hit you with paywalls when you tried to do anything useful.

My goal was to get this bot running for free. The stack in this guide is what I landed on after cutting through all the noise. The bot.py code will run on any platform that can run Python — Railway, Fly.io, Heroku, a VPS — it doesn't care where it lives.

✅ Bottom Line

Use what works for you. This guide is optimised for: (1) free, (2) beginner-friendly, (3) actually works. Different priorities? Swap the pieces out. The core bot logic stays the same.

Build Your AI Brain Bot — Workbook
8
Q3 — Once I've built it, does the bot update automatically when I create new content?

No — the bot doesn't update itself. When you create new content, you'll need to re-run the process to add it to the brain.

The good news: you don't start from scratch. Add new content to your all.txt file and re-run distill_brain.py, then re-upload to Supabase. Keep a running all.txt that you update over time and re-distill every few months.

🔄 Quick Update Cycle

New content → add to all.txt → run distill_brain.py → run upload_brain.py → done. The bot picks up the new knowledge on its next restart.

Q4 — Is my content private? Can anyone with my bot's username use it?

Two separate privacy questions — here's the honest answer on both.

Your brain database: Lives in your own Supabase and Cloudflare R2 accounts. Nobody else has access unless you give them your API keys.

Who can talk to your bot: By default, yes — anyone who finds your bot's username (e.g. @joshuaroyai_bot) can start a conversation. The token controls the bot via API. The username is public.

Most people don't worry about this. The bot answers from YOUR content — it's essentially a public knowledge assistant for your work. If you need true privacy, add a user ID whitelist to bot.py. It's about 10 lines of code — ask Claude or ChatGPT to help you add it.

⚠️ The Groq Caveat

When a user asks your bot a question, that message gets sent to Groq's servers along with relevant chunks from your brain. For most coaches using seminar transcripts and frameworks — not an issue. For highly sensitive client data — worth being aware of.

Q5 — What if Render's free tier changes, or my bot goes offline?

Two answers. First — the cron job handles downtime. With cron-job.org pinging every 10 minutes, Render never spins down. Second — your code is completely portable. Drop it onto Railway, Fly.io, or any Python-friendly host and it runs exactly the same.

🔍 The "Versus" Search Trick

Type into any search engine: render versus (leave the second field blank) and press Enter. You'll instantly see every major competitor compared. Works for any service: supabase versus, github versus, cloudflare versus. Find a replacement in under 60 seconds.

✅ Bottom Line

You're not locked in to anything. That was a deliberate design choice. Your bot.py code will run anywhere Python runs.
